places.name: Pujol
places.address: Tennyson 133, Polanco IV Secc, Miguel Hidalgo, 11550 CDMX
places.reviewSummary: Enrique Olvera's landmark restaurant that put modern Mexican cuisine on the world map. Famous for the mole madre aged for years.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers rave about the inventive tasting menu and service, though some note it is pricey and hard to book.
places.localTip: Book the taco omakase bar instead of the dining room for a more intimate, slightly easier seat.
places.story: When Pujol opened in 2000, no one imagined a Mexican kitchen would soon top global lists. Its signature mole madre is reheated and added to daily, some batches now over a thousand days old. Eating it is like tasting time itself.
places.highlight: Don't miss the mole madre, mole nuevo.

places.name: El Huequito
places.reviewSummary: A taquería institution open since 1959, beloved for its distinctive al pastor served in tacos and tortas with a secret salsa.
places.googleReviewSummary: Regulars praise the unique pastor and signature green salsa, though the original branch is small and often crowded.
places.localTip: Order the tacos al pastor 'con todo' and ask for extra of their famous salsa verde.
places.story: El Huequito means 'the little hole' — that's literally how the first stand started in 1959. Its al pastor differs from most: pork is marinated, cooked, then chopped fine and crowned with that emerald salsa. Three generations later, the recipe hasn't budged.

places.name: Contramar
places.price: $$ — lunch is the scene, no dinner
places.reviewSummary: Gabriela Cámara's airy seafood spot, the long-lunch heart of Roma. The tuna tostadas and split-color pescado a la talla are legendary.
places.googleReviewSummary: Diners love the fresh seafood and buzzy midday energy, but warn that without a reservation the wait can be long.
places.localTip: Come for a long lunch around 2pm and order the pescado a la talla to share, half red, half green.
places.story: Contramar opened in 1998 and quietly rewrote how Mexico City lunches. Its butterflied fish, painted red with chile and green with parsley, became so iconic that copies appear citywide. Yet the original still feels like a perpetual afternoon party.
places.highlight: Don't miss the tuna tostadas.

places.name: Tacos Los Especiales
places.reviewSummary: A hole-in-the-wall on bustling Madero serving steamed 'basket tacos' — soft, oily, deeply traditional street food.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors call them addictive and absurdly cheap, though seating is essentially nonexistent — it's a stand-and-eat spot.
places.localTip: Get the mole and chicharrón prensado tacos and douse them in the salsa verde on the counter.
places.story: Tacos de canasta are 'basket tacos,' pre-made and steamed under cloth until pillowy and rich. This Madero counter has fed downtown office workers for decades. There's no menu fuss — you point, you eat, you keep walking.

places.name: Expendio de Maíz Sin Nombre
places.price: $$ — no menu, you eat what they cook
places.reviewSummary: A tiny counter celebrating heirloom corn, where the day's dishes appear one by one until you say stop. Pure masa worship.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests adore the surprise format and incredible tortillas, but note it's cramped and the no-menu concept confuses some.
places.localTip: Go hungry and just say 'sí' to each plate until you're full — that's how the place works.
places.story: There's no name on the door, no printed menu, just a wood fire and native corn. Dishes arrive sequentially and you decide when to stop. It's a love letter to maíz, the grain that built Mesoamerica.
places.highlight: Don't miss the just-pressed blue-corn tortillas.

places.name: Carnitas El Bajío
places.reviewSummary: Beloved purveyor of Michoacán-style carnitas, copper-cauldron pork sold by weight for Sunday family feasts.
places.googleReviewSummary: Fans praise the tender, crispy pork and generous portions, though it's a trek from the tourist core and busiest on weekends.
places.localTip: Ask for 'surtido' to get a mix of cuts, and grab fresh tortillas and salsa to assemble your own.
places.story: Carnitas are simmered slowly in their own fat in giant copper pots until tender and golden. In Mexico City, Sunday means lining up with a container to bring home a kilo. El Bajío is where many families have done exactly that for years.

places.name: El Cardenal
places.reviewSummary: A genteel temple of traditional Mexican breakfast — hot chocolate, sweet breads, and refined antojitos in an old downtown building.
places.googleReviewSummary: Loved for the nata, fresh conchas, and impeccable service; some say it's touristy and worth arriving early to skip waits.
places.localTip: Order the pan dulce basket with hot chocolate and the nata (clotted cream) — a quintessentially Chilango breakfast.
places.story: El Cardenal turned breakfast into ceremony: steaming chocolate whisked to a froth, sweet breads still warm, and nata served like a delicacy. Generations of capital families consider Sunday morning here a tradition. It feels like stepping into a gentler century.
places.highlight: Don't miss the warm nata with conchas.

places.name: Los Cocuyos
places.reviewSummary: A famous downtown taco stand bubbling with cauldrons of suadero, longaniza, and offal cuts, busy deep into the night.
places.googleReviewSummary: Anthony Bourdain helped make it famous; reviewers love the suadero but caution the adventurous cuts aren't for everyone.
places.localTip: Try the campechano (mixed meats) and don't be shy about the spicy salsas lined up on the counter.
places.story: Los Cocuyos gained fame when Anthony Bourdain devoured a suadero taco here on camera. The cuts range from familiar to fearless — tripe, cheek, tongue — all sizzling in a vat by the sidewalk. It's downtown Mexico City at its most unfiltered.

places.name: Café Avellaneda
places.reviewSummary: A pocket-sized specialty café near Coyoacán's plaza, serious about beans and brewing methods.
places.googleReviewSummary: Coffee nerds praise the quality and knowledgeable baristas, though the space is famously small with limited seating.
places.localTip: Ask the barista to recommend a single-origin pour-over based on what's freshly roasted that week.
places.story: Tucked steps from Coyoacán's leafy square, Café Avellaneda treats coffee like a craft. The baristas geek out over origin and extraction in a space barely larger than a closet. It's proof that Mexico, a great coffee-growing nation, finally drinks its own beans well.

places.name: Buna
places.reviewSummary: A homegrown Roma roaster sourcing Mexican beans, popular with the neighborhood's creative crowd.
places.googleReviewSummary: Regulars love the espresso and minimalist vibe; some note seating fills fast on weekend mornings.
places.localTip: Grab a bag of their Chiapas or Oaxaca beans to take home — the roast is excellent.
places.story: Buna champions Mexican coffee from Chiapas, Oaxaca, and Veracruz, roasting it right here in Roma. The café became a daily ritual for the neighborhood's designers and writers. Sip slowly and you'll understand why locals are proud of homegrown beans.

places.name: Panadería Rosetta
places.reviewSummary: Chef Elena Reygadas's bakery offshoot, famous for guava rolls and concha pan dulce in a romantic Roma townhouse.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors call the guava roll life-changing but warn about long lines and a frequently packed little space.
places.localTip: Arrive before 10am for the guava-rose roll before it sells out, then take it to a bench nearby.
places.story: Born from the acclaimed restaurant Rosetta, this bakery turned a simple roll of bread, guava, and rose into a citywide obsession. Elena Reygadas, named the world's best female chef, blends European technique with Mexican soul. The smell alone draws a queue.
places.highlight: Don't miss the rol de guayaba.

places.name: Quentin Café
places.address: Av. Álvaro Obregón 64, Roma Nte., Cuauhtémoc, 06700 CDMX
places.reviewSummary: A stylish little coffee bar on Roma's grand avenue, good for a quick expertly-pulled espresso and people-watching.
places.googleReviewSummary: Praised for friendly baristas and solid coffee; a few mention it's compact and gets crowded midmorning.
places.localTip: Take your flat white to the median benches of Álvaro Obregón to watch Roma stroll by.
places.story: Roma Norte's tree-lined Álvaro Obregón is one of the city's best strolling avenues, and Quentin sits right on it. Small but precise, it's the kind of spot where neighbors linger over a cortado. The street's statues and fountains make it a perfect pause.

places.name: Café de Tacuba
places.reviewSummary: A grand 1912 café-restaurant in a tiled colonial building, famed for chocolate, tamales, and old-world atmosphere.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the historic ambiance and traditional sweets, though some find it touristy and the food uneven.
places.localTip: Come for the hot chocolate and a tamal in the back room beneath the murals, not a full dinner.
places.story: Open since 1912, Café de Tacuba glows with Talavera tiles, oil paintings, and brass fixtures. The rock band Café Tacvba even took its name from this address. Order the chocolate and you're drinking history in a colonial mansion.

places.name: Niddo
places.reviewSummary: A sunny all-day café near the Condesa loop, beloved for brunch, baked goods, and a relaxed creative crowd.
places.googleReviewSummary: Diners praise the pancakes and pastries; the main caveat is the popular weekend wait for a table.
places.localTip: Pair brunch here with a loop walk around the oval Parque México just a block away.
places.story: Niddo sits beside Avenida Ámsterdam, a street that traces the oval of a 1920s horse-racing track turned park. The café became a brunch institution for Condesa's young professionals. Grab a table, then walk the leafy ring that gives this neighborhood its dreamy shape.

places.name: Zócalo (Plaza de la Constitución)
places.address: Plaza de la Constitución S/N, Centro, Cuauhtémoc, 06000 CDMX
places.reviewSummary: One of the largest city squares in the world, the ceremonial heart of Mexico, ringed by the cathedral and National Palace.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors are awed by the scale and giant flag; some note it can be very crowded and occasionally closed for events.
places.localTip: Catch the daily flag-lowering ceremony around 6pm when soldiers fold the enormous national flag.
places.story: The Zócalo sits atop the sacred center of Aztec Tenochtitlan, buried beneath colonial stone. A colossal flag rises and falls here each day with military pomp. Stand in the middle and you're at the literal axis of the nation.
places.highlight: Don't miss the evening flag ceremony.

places.name: Palacio de Bellas Artes
places.address: Av. Juárez S/N, Centro, Cuauhtémoc, 06050 CDMX
places.reviewSummary: A dazzling Art Nouveau and Art Deco palace housing murals by Rivera and Orozco and a famed Tiffany glass curtain.
places.googleReviewSummary: Universally praised as stunning inside and out; the only common gripe is lines and limited photo access in some halls.
places.localTip: Cross to the Sears café terrace upstairs for the classic postcard view of the palace's golden dome.
places.story: Begun in 1904 and finished in 1934, Bellas Artes sank slightly into the city's soft lake bed during construction. Inside hang Diego Rivera's defiant murals and a stained-glass stage curtain made by Tiffany. It is Mexico's grandest stage for opera, ballet, and art.
places.highlight: Don't miss Rivera's 'Man at the Crossroads' mural.

places.name: Templo Mayor
places.reviewSummary: The excavated main temple of the Aztec capital, discovered in 1978, with a superb museum of unearthed treasures.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers find it moving and historically rich; some note the ruins are partly weathered and signage is mostly in Spanish.
places.localTip: Save time for the on-site museum — the Coyolxauhqui stone and Eagle Warrior statue are the real highlights.
places.story: In 1978, electrical workers struck a vast carved stone of a dismembered goddess, revealing the buried Templo Mayor beneath downtown. This was the spiritual heart of Tenochtitlan, where Aztecs believed the world began. Walking its excavated platforms, you stand inside a lost empire.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Coyolxauhqui monolith.

places.name: Teotihuacán
places.neighborhood: San Juan Teotihuacán, State of Mexico
places.reviewSummary: The colossal ancient city of the Pyramids of the Sun and Moon, an hour outside the capital and a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
places.googleReviewSummary: Awe-inspiring scale and history; visitors warn it gets hot with little shade and recommend going early.
places.localTip: Arrive at opening to beat heat and crowds, and consider a sunrise hot-air-balloon ride over the pyramids.
places.story: Teotihuacán was already an ancient ruin when the Aztecs found it; they named it 'the place where the gods were created.' At its height around 500 CE it was one of the largest cities on Earth. No one fully knows who built it, which only deepens its mystery.
places.highlight: Don't miss the view from the Pyramid of the Moon.

places.name: Castillo de Chapultepec
places.address: Bosque de Chapultepec I Secc, Miguel Hidalgo, 11100 CDMX
places.reviewSummary: A hilltop castle and former imperial residence with sweeping city views, now the National History Museum.
places.googleReviewSummary: Loved for the gardens, murals, and panorama; the climb up the hill and weekend crowds are common complaints.
places.localTip: Go up the hill on foot or by the small train, and don't miss the rooftop garden terrace for the best views.
places.story: Chapultepec Castle is the only true castle in North America to have housed sovereigns — Emperor Maximilian and Empress Carlota lived here in the 1860s. It crowns 'grasshopper hill,' sacred since Aztec times. Inside, dramatic murals tell the story of Mexico's struggles for independence.

places.name: Xochimilco
places.reviewSummary: Ancient canals and floating gardens where colorful trajinera boats drift past mariachis, food vendors, and chinampas.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors call it festive and unique; some warn boat prices should be negotiated and weekends get rowdy.
places.localTip: Agree on the per-hour boat price up front, and bring cash for the floating taco and michelada vendors.
places.story: Xochimilco's canals are the last living trace of the great lake the Aztecs built their city upon. Farmers still grow flowers on chinampas, artificial islands woven from reeds and mud. Glide along on a painted trajinera and you're sailing through five centuries of history.
places.highlight: Don't miss the eerie Island of the Dolls boat detour.

places.name: Museo Nacional de Antropología
places.address: Av. Paseo de la Reforma s/n, Polanco, Miguel Hidalgo, 11560 CDMX
places.reviewSummary: One of the world's great museums, holding the Aztec Sun Stone, Maya treasures, and a vast survey of ancient Mexico.
places.googleReviewSummary: Almost universally praised as a must-see; the main caveat is its sheer size — plan a focused visit.
places.localTip: Start with the Mexica (Aztec) hall to see the Sun Stone, then pick two more rooms rather than rushing all of them.
places.story: Beneath a giant stone umbrella fountain, this museum gathers the masterpieces of every ancient Mexican civilization. Its centerpiece is the Aztec Sun Stone, a calendar of cosmic time carved from basalt. You could spend days here and still not see it all.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Aztec Sun Stone.

places.name: Catedral Metropolitana
places.address: Plaza de la Constitución S/N, Centro, Cuauhtémoc, 06000 CDMX
places.reviewSummary: The largest cathedral in the Americas, a 250-year build mixing Baroque and Neoclassical, looming over the Zócalo.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors marvel at the gilded altars; some note it visibly tilts and sinks, and services limit access at times.
places.localTip: Take the guided bell-tower tour for rooftop views over the Zócalo and a close look at the great bells.
places.story: Built atop the ruins of an Aztec temple over nearly 250 years, the cathedral fuses every Spanish style that passed through. It slowly sinks into the soft former lakebed, and engineers fought for decades to stabilize it. Inside, the Altar of the Kings drips with gold leaf.

places.name: Parque México
places.address: Av. México s/n, Hipódromo, Cuauhtémoc, 06100 CDMX
places.reviewSummary: A lush Art Deco oval park, the green heart of Condesa, full of dog-walkers, joggers, and skating kids.
places.googleReviewSummary: Locals and visitors love the leafy calm and dogs; the only knock is that paths get busy on weekends.
places.localTip: Come at golden hour, grab an ice cream, and watch the impromptu dog social hour near the fountain.
places.story: Parque México traces the oval of an old horse-racing track, the Hipódromo, from the 1920s. Its curving Art Deco bandstand and fountains make it feel like a film set. On any evening it's the beating green heart of fashionable Condesa.

places.name: Cantina Tío Pepe
places.reviewSummary: One of downtown's oldest cantinas, all dark wood and swinging doors, pouring tequila and mezcal since the 1860s.
places.googleReviewSummary: Patrons love the authentic, time-warp atmosphere; some say service is gruff and it's smoky and small.
places.localTip: Order a tequila with a side of sangrita and soak up the genuinely old-Mexico cantina vibe.
places.story: Tío Pepe claims to be among the oldest surviving cantinas in the historic center, with swinging saloon doors and worn wood. For over a century men have argued politics and football over tequila here. It's a living relic of cantina culture, where little has changed.

places.name: Jardín Centenario
places.address: Jardín Centenario, Villa Coyoacán, Coyoacán, 04000 CDMX
places.reviewSummary: Coyoacán's beloved coyote-fountain plaza, surrounded by cafés, cantinas, and a leafy colonial atmosphere.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors adore the village feel and street life; weekends bring crowds and lively but busy vendor stalls.
places.localTip: Buy an esquite or churro from the carts, then sit by the coyote fountain to watch the weekend buskers.
places.story: Coyoacán means 'place of coyotes' in Nahuatl, honored by the twin-coyote fountain at the plaza's center. Once a separate village, it kept its cobblestones and slow pace as the city swallowed it. This is where Chilangos come to feel the city slow down.
places.highlight: Don't miss the coyote fountain.

places.name: Mercado de Coyoacán
places.address: Ignacio Allende s/n, Villa Coyoacán, Coyoacán, 04000 CDMX
places.reviewSummary: A vibrant covered market famous for tostadas piled high, plus crafts, flowers, and produce stalls.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers rave about the tostadas; the maze of stalls can feel crowded and overwhelming at peak hours.
places.localTip: Head straight to the Tostadas Coyoacán stalls and order one heaped with ceviche or tinga.
places.story: Locals make pilgrimages to this market for one thing above all: tostadas, crisp tortillas mounded with seafood and salsas. Beyond the food counters lie aisles of piñatas, papel picado, and dried chiles. It's a sensory crash course in everyday Mexican life.
places.highlight: Don't miss the famous tostadas.

places.name: Mercado de San Juan
places.reviewSummary: A gourmet and exotic-foods market where chefs source rare ingredients, cheeses, and even unusual game meats.
places.googleReviewSummary: Foodies love the variety and tastings; some find the exotic-meat stalls jarring and prices higher than other markets.
places.localTip: Let a cheese or charcuterie vendor build you a tasting plate to eat at one of the standing bars.
places.story: Mercado de San Juan has been the city's pantry for gourmets and chefs for generations, stocking everything from imported cheeses to crocodile and ant eggs. Vendors press samples on you the moment you slow down. It's where Mexico City's culinary curiosity runs wildest.

places.name: Cafebrería El Péndulo
places.address: Álvaro Obregón 86, Roma Nte., Cuauhtémoc, 06700 CDMX
places.reviewSummary: A beloved bookstore-café chain, its Roma branch a plant-filled, balconied haven for reading and brunch.
places.googleReviewSummary: Loved for the cozy literary atmosphere and live music nights; some say food is secondary to the ambiance.
places.localTip: Go up to the leafy mezzanine with a coffee and a new book — they don't rush you to leave.
places.story: El Péndulo blends a serious bookstore with a café and stage, wrapping readers in greenery and warm light. The Roma branch hosts live music and poetry under its skylights. It's the kind of place Chilangos disappear into for an entire rainy afternoon.

places.name: Arena México
places.reviewSummary: The 'Cathedral of Lucha Libre,' where masked wrestlers battle several nights a week before roaring crowds.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors call it wildly fun and theatrical; tips include avoiding the surrounding area late at night and buying real tickets.
places.localTip: Buy an official mask from vendors outside and sit in the ringside lower section for the full spectacle.
places.story: Lucha libre is part sport, part soap opera, where good 'técnicos' battle villainous 'rudos' in flying acrobatics and sequined masks. Arena México has been its grand temple since 1956. The crowd's chants and insults are half the show.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Friday-night main events.

places.name: Bósforo
places.reviewSummary: A dim, candlelit mezcalería downtown pouring rare small-batch mezcals from tiny producers across Mexico.
places.googleReviewSummary: Mezcal lovers praise the curated selection and vibe; the dark, cramped space and cash-only policy are common notes.
places.localTip: Tell the bartender your taste and let them guide you through a flight of unusual wild-agave mezcals.
places.story: Bósforo looks like a secret — a candlelit hole-in-the-wall pouring mezcals you'll find nowhere else. Each one tells of a specific village, agave, and family distiller. Sip slowly: good mezcal is meant to be kissed, not shot.

places.name: Casa Jacaranda
places.reviewSummary: A renowned home cooking class that starts with a market tour and ends with a full Mexican feast you cooked.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests call it a highlight of their trip; the only caveat is it's pricey and books out well in advance.
places.localTip: Book weeks ahead and come hungry — you'll shop, cook, and then eat everything you made.
places.story: Casa Jacaranda begins with a stroll through a local market to gather chiles, herbs, and produce. Back in a beautiful Roma home, hosts teach you to grind, blend, and build classic dishes from scratch. By the end you've made — and devoured — a proper Mexican banquet.

places.name: Volare Globos (Teotihuacán)
places.address: Carr. Teotihuacán s/n, San Juan Teotihuacán, 55800 Méx.
places.reviewSummary: Sunrise hot-air-balloon flights drifting over the pyramids of Teotihuacán — a bucket-list aerial view.
places.googleReviewSummary: Riders describe it as magical and well-organized; the very early start and weather cancellations are common notes.
places.localTip: Book the earliest sunrise slot for calm air and golden light over the Pyramid of the Sun.
places.story: There's no better way to grasp Teotihuacán's scale than floating above it at dawn. As the sun rises, the Avenue of the Dead and the great pyramids glow gold below. It's a silent, almost sacred few minutes that travelers never forget.
places.highlight: Don't miss the dawn light on the Pyramid of the Sun.

places.name: Eat Mexico Street Food Tour
places.address: Meeting point near Zócalo, Centro, Cuauhtémoc, 06000 CDMX
places.reviewSummary: A well-loved guided crawl through downtown stalls and markets, demystifying tacos, quesadillas, and antojitos.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests praise knowledgeable guides and safe, delicious picks; some note there's a lot of walking and you'll be very full.
places.localTip: Skip breakfast — these tours pack in many tastings, and pace yourself on the early stops.
places.story: Mexico City's street food is bewilderingly deep, and a good guide unlocks it fast. You'll learn which stand makes the best suadero and why the busiest cart is always the safest. It turns sidewalk eating from intimidating into addictive.

places.name: Pulquería Las Duelistas
places.reviewSummary: A psychedelic-muraled pulquería serving the ancient fermented agave drink in fruit-flavored 'curados.'
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the funky vibe and cheap curados; the slimy texture of pulque divides first-timers.
places.localTip: Start with a sweet fruit curado (guava or oat) rather than plain pulque if it's your first time.
places.story: Pulque is the milky, fermented sap of the maguey, drunk in Mexico since long before the Spanish arrived. Once nearly extinct, it's roaring back among young Chilangos. Beneath Las Duelistas's neon murals, you sip a drink the Aztecs called sacred.

places.name: Museo Frida Kahlo (Casa Azul)
places.reviewSummary: The cobalt-blue house where Frida Kahlo was born, lived, and died, now a museum of her art, belongings, and life.
places.googleReviewSummary: Deeply moving for fans; the main complaint is that tickets must be booked far ahead and lines are long.
places.localTip: Buy timed tickets online days in advance — walk-ups are often turned away, especially on weekends.
places.story: Frida Kahlo was born in this bright blue house in 1907 and died here in 1954, and her presence still saturates every room. You'll see her wheelchair at the easel, her corsets, and the kitchen painted in folk colors. It's not just a museum — it's her world, preserved.
places.highlight: Don't miss her studio and the bed with the mirror above it.

places.name: Museo Soumaya
places.address: Blvd. Miguel de Cervantes Saavedra 303, Granada, Miguel Hidalgo, 11529 CDMX
places.reviewSummary: A shimmering silver-tiled building by Plaza Carso holding Carlos Slim's vast art collection, including many Rodins.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the free entry and striking architecture; some find the collection's curation uneven.
places.localTip: Take the elevator to the top floor and spiral down through the galleries — the Rodin hall is the finale.
places.story: The Soumaya's mirrored, hourglass form is one of the city's most photographed buildings. Inside sits one of the largest Rodin collections outside France, gathered by billionaire Carlos Slim. Best of all, admission is completely free.
places.highlight: Don't miss the top-floor Rodin sculptures.

places.name: Museo Jumex
places.address: Blvd. Miguel de Cervantes Saavedra 303, Granada, Miguel Hidalgo, 11529 CDMX
places.reviewSummary: Mexico's leading contemporary art museum, a sawtooth-roofed gem facing the Soumaya in Plaza Carso.
places.googleReviewSummary: Art fans praise the world-class exhibitions and building; casual visitors sometimes find shows too conceptual.
places.localTip: Pair it with the Soumaya next door — two great museums in one easy stop.
places.story: Funded by a juice fortune, Museo Jumex brings blue-chip contemporary art to Mexico City in a building by David Chipperfield. Its sawtooth roof bathes the galleries in soft northern light. Across the plaza glitters the Soumaya, making this corner an art-lover's two-for-one.

places.name: Palacio Nacional Murals
places.address: Plaza de la Constitución s/n, Centro, Cuauhtémoc, 06000 CDMX
places.reviewSummary: Diego Rivera's epic stairway murals depicting the entire history of Mexico, inside the seat of government.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors are awed by the murals' scale and detail; access depends on security and ID, and hours can be unpredictable.
places.localTip: Bring a passport or ID for the security check, and look closely for Rivera's hidden portraits in the crowds.
places.story: Across the grand staircase of the National Palace, Diego Rivera painted Mexico's entire saga — from Aztec markets to revolution. He filled the walls with real figures, heroes and villains alike. It's a history book you read by walking up the stairs.
places.highlight: Don't miss the central 'Epic of the Mexican People' mural.

places.name: Casa Estudio Diego Rivera y Frida Kahlo
places.address: Diego Rivera 2, San Ángel Inn, Álvaro Obregón, 01060 CDMX
places.reviewSummary: The bold modernist twin studios designed by Juan O'Gorman for Rivera and Kahlo, joined by a rooftop bridge.
places.googleReviewSummary: Architecture and art fans love it; some say it's smaller and quieter than the Casa Azul, which suits or disappoints.
places.localTip: Combine it with a stroll through San Ángel's cobbled streets and the Saturday Bazaar Sábado.
places.story: In 1932 architect Juan O'Gorman built Rivera and Kahlo two separate Bauhaus-style houses — one blue, one red — linked by a slender bridge. The couple's stormy marriage played out across that gap. It's one of the first functionalist buildings in the Americas.

places.name: Biblioteca Central UNAM
places.reviewSummary: The UNAM campus library, its windowless tower wrapped in Juan O'Gorman's giant stone mosaic murals.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors are stunned by the mosaic façade; some note the campus is large and it's a metro ride from the center.
places.localTip: Walk all four sides of the tower — each mosaic face tells a different chapter of Mexican history.
places.story: The Central Library's tower is sheathed in millions of colored stones, forming murals of Aztec, colonial, and modern Mexico. Juan O'Gorman built the entire UNESCO-listed campus as a manifesto of Mexican modernism. No camera quite captures standing beneath it.
places.highlight: Don't miss the north façade depicting the pre-Hispanic world.

places.name: Museo Tamayo
places.address: Paseo de la Reforma 51, Bosque de Chapultepec I Secc, Miguel Hidalgo, 11580 CDMX
places.reviewSummary: A sleek concrete museum in Chapultepec park dedicated to modern and contemporary art, founded by Rufino Tamayo.
places.googleReviewSummary: Praised for the architecture and rotating shows; some find the permanent collection smaller than expected.
places.localTip: It pairs perfectly with the Anthropology Museum across the park for an art-and-history double bill.
places.story: Painter Rufino Tamayo built this museum to share his collection of modern masters with the public. Its low concrete form nestles into Chapultepec's trees like a piece of land art. Inside you'll find Picasso and Warhol alongside Mexico's own moderns.

places.name: Plaza Garibaldi
places.reviewSummary: The legendary square where mariachi bands gather nightly, ready to play for tips beneath the neon.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the live mariachi energy; many warn to stay alert at night and go with a group or guide.
places.localTip: Agree on the price per song before the band starts, and visit the on-site Tequila & Mezcal museum.
places.story: For nearly a century, mariachis in full charro suits have congregated at Garibaldi waiting to be hired by the song. Heartbroken lovers and celebrating families alike summon them here. Hand over a few pesos and a ten-piece band serenades you on the spot.
places.highlight: Don't miss hiring a band for 'Cielito Lindo.'

places.name: Salón Los Ángeles
places.reviewSummary: A storied 1937 ballroom where live danzón, mambo, and salsa orchestras keep an old-Havana tradition alive.
places.googleReviewSummary: Dancers love the authentic big-band atmosphere; the surrounding neighborhood means taking a taxi to and from is wise.
places.localTip: Tuesday and Saturday are the big dance nights — dress up and take a cab door-to-door.
places.story: Since 1937, Salón Los Ángeles has spun under crystal chandeliers to live mambo and danzón orchestras. Its motto: 'whoever doesn't know Los Ángeles doesn't know Mexico.' Couples who've danced here for fifty years still glide across its sprung floor.

places.name: Zinco Jazz Club
places.reviewSummary: An intimate basement jazz club in a former 1920s bank vault, hosting top Mexican and visiting musicians.
places.googleReviewSummary: Patrons praise the sound, ambiance, and talent; reservations are recommended as the small room fills fast.
places.localTip: Reserve a table near the stage in advance — walk-ins often face a full house on weekends.
places.story: Down in the cellar of a 1920s bank, Zinco turned a stone vault into one of Latin America's best jazz rooms. The low ceilings wrap the music around you. Order a cocktail, settle in, and let the brass do the rest.

places.name: Sala Nezahualcóyotl
places.reviewSummary: A vineyard-style concert hall on the UNAM campus, acclaimed for its acoustics and resident philharmonic.
places.googleReviewSummary: Music lovers rave about the sound and design; it's a bit far south, so plan transport for evening concerts.
places.localTip: Catch the OFUNAM philharmonic on a Sunday afternoon for great music at student-friendly prices.
places.story: Opened in 1976, Sala Nezahualcóyotl was the first concert hall in the Americas with the orchestra surrounded by audience, vineyard-style. It's named for the poet-king of ancient Texcoco. Acousticians still rank its sound among the continent's finest.

places.name: Foro Indie Rocks!
places.reviewSummary: A mid-size Roma venue at the center of the city's indie and alternative live-music scene.
places.googleReviewSummary: Concertgoers like the sound and lineup of emerging acts; some mention it gets hot and packed for popular shows.
places.localTip: Check their calendar for up-and-coming Latin American bands before they blow up internationally.
places.story: Foro Indie Rocks! became a launchpad for Mexico's alternative bands and a magnet for touring indie acts. On any given night Roma's music-loving youth pack its floor. It's where you catch tomorrow's headliners in a sweaty, joyful crush.

places.name: Licorería Limantour
places.address: Av. Álvaro Obregón 106, Roma Nte., Cuauhtémoc, 06700 CDMX
places.reviewSummary: A perennial World's 50 Best Bars pick, the standard-bearer of Mexico City's cocktail renaissance since 2011.
places.googleReviewSummary: Cocktail lovers praise inventive drinks and service; the two-level space can get crowded, so come early or reserve.
places.localTip: Order the Margarita Al Pastor — a savory taco-inspired twist that's the bar's signature.
places.story: When Limantour opened in 2011, it helped ignite Mexico City's now-legendary cocktail scene. Its Margarita Al Pastor reimagines a taco as a drink, with chile, pineapple, and cilantro. Year after year it lands on the world's best-bars list — and earns it.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Margarita Al Pastor.

places.name: Hanky Panky Cocktail Bar
places.reviewSummary: A hidden speakeasy entered through a secret door, regularly ranked among the world's best bars.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the theater of the hidden entrance and the drinks; reservations are essential and it's intimate.
places.localTip: Reserve ahead — they'll send the secret location and entry instructions only after you book.
places.story: Hanky Panky hides behind an unmarked door you must be told about, classic Prohibition theater. Inside, bartenders craft serious cocktails in a snug, glamorous room. The mystery of finding it is half the fun.

places.name: Supra Roma (Terraza)
places.address: Av. Álvaro Obregón 93, Roma Nte., Cuauhtémoc, 06700 CDMX
places.reviewSummary: A lively rooftop terrace over Roma with city views, craft beer, and a young weekend crowd.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors enjoy the open-air vibe and sunset views; some note long waits to get up to the roof on busy nights.
places.localTip: Come for sunset before the crowds arrive to grab a rail seat overlooking the avenue.
places.story: Roma's rooftops are where the neighborhood's nightlife spills upward toward the open sky. Supra's terrace fills with a buzzing young crowd as the sun drops behind the city. It's a perfect first stop before a night out below.

places.name: Yu Yu
places.reviewSummary: A fashionable house and disco club in a Juárez mansion, a hub of the city's stylish late-night scene.
places.googleReviewSummary: Clubbers love the music and crowd; expect a door policy, lines, and steeper drink prices.
places.localTip: Go after midnight and dress sharp — the door curates a fashion-forward crowd.
places.story: Set in a converted Juárez mansion, Yu Yu draws Mexico City's design-and-music crowd for late nights of house and disco. The old-house bones give it a glamour most clubs lack. Come late; here the night truly starts after midnight.

places.name: La Clandestina
places.reviewSummary: A rustic Condesa mezcalería pouring small-batch mezcals in a warm, wood-and-tin atmosphere.
places.googleReviewSummary: Mezcal fans praise the curated bottles and knowledgeable staff; the small space gets loud and packed on weekends.
places.localTip: Ask for a tasting of three regional mezcals and sip them neat with orange and sal de gusano.
places.story: La Clandestina treats mezcal like wine, pouring rare bottlings from family palenques across Oaxaca and beyond. The rough-luxe room feels like a country distillery dropped into Condesa. Each glass carries the smoke of a wood-fired pit oven.

places.name: La Ópera Bar
places.address: Av. 5 de Mayo 10, Centro, Cuauhtémoc, 06000 CDMX
places.reviewSummary: An opulent 1895 cantina with carved wood, gilt ceilings, and a famous bullet hole said to be Pancho Villa's.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the gorgeous belle-époque interior and history; some find the food average and prices touristy.
places.localTip: Ask the waiter to point out the bullet hole in the ceiling, then toast with a tequila beneath the gilded mirrors.
places.story: La Ópera has poured drinks since 1895 beneath ornate gold-leaf ceilings and beveled mirrors. Legend says Pancho Villa rode in on horseback and fired a shot still visible in the ceiling. It's the most beautiful place downtown to nurse a tequila.
places.highlight: Don't miss Pancho Villa's bullet hole in the ceiling.

places.name: Departamento
places.reviewSummary: An apartment-turned-club spinning house and techno for the city's underground electronic crowd.
places.googleReviewSummary: Dancers praise the DJs and intimate vibe; the small capacity means lines and an early door cutoff on big nights.
places.localTip: Follow them on social media for the lineup — the best nights book out and the door closes early.
places.story: Departamento took the literal shape of an apartment and turned it into a tight, sweaty temple for house and techno. The low-key setting keeps it feeling like a private party. For Mexico City's electronic-music heads, it's a beloved late-night haunt.

places.name: Cervecería Hércules / Patio
places.reviewSummary: A relaxed craft-beer spot in Condesa pouring Mexican microbrews on a lively, leafy street.
places.googleReviewSummary: Beer fans enjoy the rotating taps and chill patio; some find it crowded and a touch pricey on weekends.
places.localTip: Grab a flight of Mexican craft beers and a sidewalk table on Tamaulipas to watch Condesa go by.
places.story: Mexico's craft-beer wave reached full froth in Condesa, where bars like this pour ambers and IPAs brewed across the country. The tree-lined streets invite long, slow evenings outdoors. It's where locals trade tequila for a cold artisanal pint.

places.name: Casa Sauza / Mezcal Tasting Labs (Roma)
places.reviewSummary: Guided agave-spirits tastings teaching the differences between tequila, mezcal, and rarer distillates.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests find the sessions informative and fun; some wish they were longer for the price.
places.localTip: Eat beforehand and pace yourself — a proper agave tasting moves through many strong pours.
places.story: Agave spirits are a whole universe beyond the party shot: each maguey, region, and roasting method changes everything. A guided tasting unlocks that complexity glass by glass. You'll leave knowing why mezcal is sipped, never slammed.

places.name: Tout Chocolat Workshop
places.reviewSummary: A chocolatier offering hands-on workshops tracing cacao from the Mesoamerican bean to modern bonbons.
places.googleReviewSummary: Participants love making their own chocolates and the history lesson; classes are small and book up.
places.localTip: Book a small-group session and ask about the history of cacao as Aztec currency.
places.story: Chocolate began here: the Aztecs prized cacao so highly they used the beans as money. A workshop walks you from raw bean to tempered bonbon, with plenty of tasting along the way. You'll never look at a chocolate bar the same way again.

places.name: Taller Ceramica La Esquina
places.reviewSummary: A welcoming Roma ceramics studio offering wheel-throwing and hand-building classes for all levels.
places.googleReviewSummary: Students praise patient instructors and a relaxed pace; spots are limited so booking ahead is advised.
places.localTip: Wear clothes you don't mind dirtying and book a beginner wheel session to make a keepsake bowl.
places.story: Mexico has a deep ceramic tradition, from Talavera tiles to barro negro pottery. A hands-on class lets you feel that clay heritage spin under your own palms. You'll go home with something imperfect, earthy, and entirely yours.

places.name: La Cocina de San Juan Class
places.reviewSummary: A market-to-table cooking workshop based at Mercado de San Juan, mastering salsas, moles, and tortillas.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love shopping the market then cooking the haul; classes are intimate and fill quickly.
places.localTip: Come hungry and ask to learn a salsa technique you can recreate at home with easy-to-find chiles.
places.story: Mexican cooking is built on the molcajete, the comal, and the alchemy of dried chiles. Starting in the famous San Juan market, you gather ingredients then transform them by hand. You'll grind your own salsa and press tortillas from fresh masa.

places.name: Ángel de la Independencia
places.address: Paseo de la Reforma s/n, Juárez, Cuauhtémoc, 06600 CDMX
places.reviewSummary: The golden 'Angel' victory column on grand Reforma avenue, the city's emblem and celebration gathering point.
places.googleReviewSummary: A beloved photo icon; the busy traffic circle means careful crossing, and tower access is occasional.
places.localTip: Shoot at blue hour when the gilded angel catches the last light against Reforma's skyscrapers.
places.story: Raised in 1910 for the centennial of independence, El Ángel has become the city's good-luck totem. Mexicans flood its base to celebrate World Cup wins and ring in causes. The gilded Winged Victory atop the column gleams day and night.
places.highlight: Don't miss it lit up after dark.

places.name: Biblioteca Vasconcelos
places.address: Eje 1 Nte. s/n, Buenavista, Cuauhtémoc, 06350 CDMX
places.reviewSummary: A breathtaking 'megalibrary' of floating glass bookshelves around a suspended whale skeleton sculpture.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors call it a photographer's dream; it can be busy and the suspended walkways limit some angles.
places.localTip: Shoot from the upper floors looking down the central void for the dizzying symmetrical shot.
places.story: Opened in 2006, Biblioteca Vasconcelos seems to defy gravity, its glass shelves hanging in a vast transparent hall. Suspended at its center floats Gabriel Orozco's whale skeleton sculpture. Step inside and you feel like you've entered a sci-fi cathedral of books.
places.highlight: Don't miss the suspended whale skeleton.

places.name: Roma–Condesa Street Art Walls
places.address: Around Calle Tabasco & Orizaba, Roma Nte., Cuauhtémoc, 06700 CDMX
places.reviewSummary: The mural-splashed corners of Roma and Condesa, where building-sized street art turns walks into galleries.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors enjoy the vibrant murals and café-hopping; the best pieces are scattered, so a little wandering is needed.
places.localTip: Wander Orizaba and Tabasco streets on foot — the most striking murals reward those who explore on foot.
places.story: Roma and Condesa wear their creativity on their walls, with massive murals by Mexican and international street artists. A simple stroll becomes an open-air gallery. Turn each corner and a new burst of color waits to be framed.

places.name: Torre Latinoamericana Mirador
places.address: Eje Central Lázaro Cárdenas 2, Centro, Cuauhtémoc, 06000 CDMX
places.reviewSummary: A mid-century skyscraper with a 44th-floor observation deck offering 360-degree views over the megacity.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the panorama, especially at sunset; haze can limit visibility and the deck gets crowded.
places.localTip: Go an hour before sunset to catch both daylight and the city lights coming on from the open-air deck.
places.story: When it opened in 1956, the Torre Latinoamericana was a marvel — a skyscraper engineered to ride out earthquakes on the soft lakebed, and it has, repeatedly. From the top deck the city sprawls endlessly toward distant volcanoes. On clear days you can see Popocatépetl smoking on the horizon.
places.highlight: Don't miss sunset over the historic center.

places.name: Casa de los Azulejos
places.reviewSummary: An 18th-century palace sheathed entirely in blue-and-white Talavera tiles, now home to a Sanborns café.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors admire the dazzling tiled exterior and courtyard; the inside café is touristy and can be busy.
places.localTip: Step into the covered courtyard to photograph the Orozco mural on the grand staircase.
places.story: The 'House of Tiles' wraps an entire 1700s mansion in blue-and-white Puebla Talavera, a flex of colonial wealth. Inside, a José Clemente Orozco mural crowns the staircase. The contrast of folk tile and fine art makes it endlessly photogenic.

places.name: Lago Mayor Chapultepec (Fuente Monumental)
places.address: Bosque de Chapultepec II Secc, Miguel Hidalgo, 11100 CDMX
places.reviewSummary: A scenic lake in Chapultepec's second section with paddle boats, fountains, and reflective city views.
places.googleReviewSummary: Families love the relaxed paddle-boating and greenery; weekends are busy and some areas feel worn.
places.localTip: Rent a paddle boat in the late afternoon for reflections of the trees and fountain on the water.
places.story: Chapultepec is one of the largest city parks in the Western Hemisphere, twice the size of New York's Central Park. Its lakes and monumental fountain offer a rare mirror of calm in the megacity. Rent a boat and the skyline ripples across the water at your feet.
